5 min read
Just now

--

Part 2 of 11 — Building a Production AI Portfolio Copilot
In Part 1, I covered how the main PydanticAI agent is wired up with typed tools and dependency injection. That agent handles most portfolio questions well. “What are my holdings?” “How is AAPL doing?” “What is my cash balance?” These are single-step lookups. One tool call, one answer.
But then a user asks: “Find me undervalued dividend stocks in healthcare.” Or “Compare AAPL, MSFT, and GOOGL on fundamentals.” Or “Which sectors are leading the market this quarter?”
These questions are different. They require screening across 500+ stocks, pulling multiple data points, running comparisons, and sometimes searching the web for qualitative context. If I crammed all of that into the main agent, the system prompt would be massive, the tool list unwieldy, and the failure modes tangled.
So I split the work across two agents.
The Orchestrator-Specialist Pattern
The main agent (the orchestrator) handles portfolio-specific questions using MCP tools. It knows your holdings, your transactions, your cash balance, and your performance history.
The research agent (the specialist) handles broad market analysis. It can screen the S&P 500 by fundamentals, compare multiple stocks side by side, rank sectors by performance, and search the web for analyst consensus.
The main agent delegates to the research agent through a single tool called research_and_analyze:
@agent.tool
async def research_and_analyze(
ctx: RunContext[AssistantDeps], question: str
) -> str:
research_agent = get_research_agent()
try:
result = await research_agent.run(question, usage=ctx.usage)
return result.output
except Exception as exc:
return (
"Research analysis is temporarily unavailable. "
"I can still answer using per-symbol tools - please ask about "
"specific tickers and I will look them up directly."
)The main agent decides when to delegate. The research agent does the work. It returns a string report. The main agent takes that report and formats the final answer for the user.
Why Two Agents Instead of One
Three concrete reasons:
Different prompts. The main agent’s system prompt is tuned for conversational portfolio advising. “Be direct. Name names. Give opinions backed by data.” The research agent’s prompt is tuned for structured equity analysis. “Start with quantitative screening. Supplement with web search. Synthesize into a thesis with bull and bear cases.” Same model, different personas.
Different tools. The main agent has 10 portfolio tools scoped to the current user. The research agent has a stock screener, a fundamentals comparator, a sector performance ranker, and web search. Zero overlap. If I merged them, the model would have 15+ tools to choose from on every call. More tools means more confusion about which to pick.
Different failure domains. If the research agent fails (rate limit, API timeout, bad screener data), the main agent still works. It catches the exception and tells the user, “Research is unavailable, but I can look up specific tickers directly.” The main agent’s core function, answering questions about your portfolio, stays intact.
The Research Agent’s Toolset
The research agent has three purpose-built tools plus web search:
@agent.tool_plain
async def screen_stocks(
sector: str = "",
min_market_cap: float = 0,
max_pe: float = 0,
min_dividend_yield: float = 0,
sort_by: str = "market_cap",
limit: int = 10,
) -> ScreenerOutput:
return await stock_screener(...)screen_stocks filters S&P 500 companies by sector, market cap, P/E ratio, dividend yield, and momentum. It returns up to 15 results with key metrics.
compare_stocks takes 2-5 ticker symbols and does a side-by-side fundamentals comparison: valuation, growth, and risk metrics.
get_sector_performance ranks all 11 GICS sector ETFs by returns over a configurable period.
These are all @agent.tool_plain - they do not need the RunContext because they have no user-specific dependencies. They query public market data, not portfolio data.
The Research Prompt
The research agent has a focused methodology baked into its system prompt:

• Start with quantitative screening or comparison using the tools.

• Supplement with web search for qualitative context — analyst consensus, upcoming catalysts, competitive position.

• Synthesize both into a clear thesis.
It also has output rules. Every recommendation must include both a bull case and a bear case. It must name specific stocks and cite specific numbers. It must use simple language a retail investor can understand.
One rule I added after launch: never mention tool names in the output. Early versions would say things like “According to the stock_screener tool…” That breaks the illusion. The user should not know or care which tools the agent used. The information should feel like it came from an analyst, not a function call.
Routing: When Does the Main Agent Delegate?
The system prompt has explicit routing rules:
Use research_and_analyze when: - The question involves screening or ranking stocks ("top 10 stocks for...") - Comparing multiple stocks against each other - Broad market or sector analysis - Investment ideas or stock discovery
Use the per-symbol MCP tools directly when: — The question is about a specific stock the user names (“how is AAPL doing?”) — The question is about the user’s own portfolio (“what are my holdings?”) — The question is about a single position (“do I own NVDA?”)
This works because the LLM is good at intent classification. “Compare AAPL and MSFT” clearly falls into the research bucket. “What is my AAPL position worth?” clearly falls into the MCP bucket. The ambiguous cases are rare.
Token Sharing via usage
Notice this line in the delegation call:
result = await research_agent.run(question, usage=ctx.usage)The usage=ctx.usage parameter lets PydanticAI track token usage across both agents in a single accounting context. Without it, the research agent's tokens would be invisible to the parent run. That matters for cost monitoring and for rate limit awareness.
Singleton Construction
Both agents use the same lazy singleton pattern:
@lru_cache(maxsize=1)
def get_research_agent() -> Agent[None, str]:
return _build_research_agent()Built once on first use. Cached for the process lifetime. If the API key is missing, only the research features degrade. The main agent and the rest of the app still work.
What the User Sees
The user does not know two agents are involved. They ask a question. They get an answer. If the question triggers delegation, the streamed response might take a few extra seconds because the research agent is doing multi-step tool calls under the hood. But the UX is identical.
I added progress stages on the frontend to handle this. When the research agent is active, the UI shows “Researching…” instead of just “Thinking…” Small touch, but it sets expectations for the longer wait.
When to Split vs When to Keep One Agent
My rule of thumb: split when the prompt, the tools, or the failure mode would be significantly different.
If two tasks need the same tools and the same prompt style, keep them in one agent. Adding agents adds latency (extra LLM calls), complexity (more error handling), and cost (more tokens for the sub-agent’s system prompt).
In my case, the split was clear. Portfolio Q&A and equity research are fundamentally different workloads. Different data sources, different reasoning patterns, different output formats. One agent trying to do both would be mediocre at each.
Takeaway
Multi-agent is not about having more agents. It is about having the right boundaries between them. Split when prompts diverge, tools do not overlap, and you want independent failure domains. Keep it simple when one agent can do the job.
The main agent knows your portfolio. The research agent knows the market. They talk through a single delegation tool with graceful fallback. That is the whole architecture.
Next up: Part 3 — MCP Server Design: Process Isolation, Security, and Context Optimization
